Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd spend about 25 hours a week on coursework, but it was scattered and unstructured. Some days I'd cram for hours, other days I'd barely look at anything. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't get myself to stick to a schedule. My anxiety made it hard to even start sometimes.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really depending on what was due and how stressed I was feeling. I'd have these bursts of activity right before deadlines, then basically disappear for days. Regular study times felt impossible to maintain with everything else going on. I kept telling myself I'd do better next week, but that rarely happened.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an assignment deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes a classmate would message me asking about something, and that would remind me I should probably check the LMS. I rarely logged in just to browse or see what was new. It felt more like a chore than anything helpful.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I never got to a point where checking the LMS felt natural or automatic. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do. I'd go days without logging in and then suddenly realize I'd missed announcements or discussions. The peer support group tried to help me stay on track, but even that didn't make it routine.
Q5: The videos were actually pretty helpful when I watched them. I'm a visual learner, so seeing concepts explained visually made more sense than reading dense text. I also found the discussion posts useful because other students would ask questions I hadn't thought of. When I actually engaged with those, I learned something.
Q6: I mostly ignored the readings and supplementary materials. They felt overwhelming and I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The optional practice quizzes also got skipped because they weren't graded. If something wasn't going to directly affect my grade, it was hard to motivate myself to do it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate payoff, like it would help me on an upcoming assignment or exam. If it was graded, I'd do it. If it helped me understand something I was confused about, that was valuable too. But if it felt like busywork or extra content, I just couldn't bring myself to care.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely different. Required activities had this weight to them, this pressure that made me actually do them even when I didn't want to. Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore. With my anxiety already high, adding more tasks that weren't mandatory felt like too much.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos and study them on my own time without being online. I also used YouTube a lot to find alternative explanations of concepts. My friends and I would study together in person, going over notes and quizzing each other. None of that showed up in the system.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing printed notes or working through problems in a notebook for hours. I'd also study with my peer group in person, discussing concepts and helping each other understand things. The LMS would just show I hadn't logged in, but I was actually putting in real effort offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then work with it offline for days. I preferred having materials saved locally so I didn't have to deal with logging in repeatedly. My internet is fine, but it just felt easier to have everything on my laptop where I could access it without the LMS.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. My anxiety spiked and I felt paralyzed by all the work piling up. I'd look at the LMS and feel overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind. The peer support helped a bit, but there were definitely weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to engage at all.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or exhausted, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars ate into my study time. Sometimes I was just too confused by the material to even know where to start, so I'd avoid logging in altogether.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just feeling too anxious to focus. I'd watch a video but realize at the end I hadn't retained any of it. Being logged in didn't mean I was actually learning.
Q15: I procrastinated on discussion posts and readings constantly, even though I knew they mattered. The anxiety made it hard to start things because I was afraid of doing them wrong. I'd tell myself I'd feel more ready tomorrow, but that just made the pile bigger and the anxiety worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd cram everything in the last day or two before something was due. My LMS activity probably spiked right before deadlines and flatlined otherwise. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure was the only thing that overcame my avoidance.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but sometimes not in a good way. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback made my anxiety worse and made me want to avoid that part of the course. I'd sometimes delay checking my grades because I was scared of what I'd see.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid, which was both good and bad. The clear deadlines helped me know what was expected, but the pacing felt relentless. If I fell behind, it was hard to catch up. More flexibility might have helped me manage my anxiety better.
Q19: The weekly modules were organized clearly, which helped when I actually engaged. But the sheer amount of content in each module felt overwhelming. Having so many required and optional activities made it hard to prioritize. Simpler, more focused modules would have been easier to handle.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to understand what's expected without actually completing work. They could be anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. Or maybe they're exploring but getting stuck on actually producing something to submit.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I didn't log in much, but I studied offline and with peers, which helped me pass my exams. My assignment completion was high even though my engagement looked low. The LMS doesn't capture everything that contributes to learning.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss offline studying, group work, and the mental effort of processing information. They also can't capture how anxiety or stress affects your ability to engage meaningfully.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just going through the motions. It's about being mentally present, asking questions, and trying to connect concepts. For me, real engagement happened in peer discussions and personal study, not always through clicking around the LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything I did in this course. It wouldn't show the peer support sessions that actually helped me get through, or the offline studying that made up for my low login frequency. It also wouldn't capture how surprised I was that I actually passed despite feeling so disconnected.
